examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: Vertex 0 unvisited → DFS(0): Mark 0 visited, explore neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited, explore neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited, explore neighbors: 1 visited. Return to main. components=1. Step 3: Vertex 1 visited, skip. Vertex 2 visited, skip. Vertex 3 unvisited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited, explore neighbors: 4 unvisited → DFS(4): Mark 4 visited, explore neighbors: 3 visited. Return to main. components=2. Step 4: Vertex 4 visited, skip. All vertices visited → return components=2.

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: Vertex 0 unvisited → DFS(0): Mark 0 visited, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited, neighbors: 1 visited, 3 unvisited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited, neighbors: 2 visited. Return to main. components=1. Step 3: Vertices 1,2,3 all visited, skip. All vertices visited → return components=1.

approaches.name: DFS/BFS Traversal
approaches.summary: Use DFS or BFS to traverse the graph, marking visited nodes to count connected components. This is a standard graph traversal method that works for any graph representation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS/BFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Given an undirected graph represented as adjacency list, count the number of connected components using DFS or BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that each connected component is a maximal set of vertices where you can travel from any vertex to any other vertex via edges. By systematically exploring the graph using DFS or BFS, we can identify all vertices belonging to the same component. The algorithm works by iterating through all vertices, and for each unvisited vertex, we initiate a DFS/BFS traversal that visits all vertices reachable from that starting point. Each such traversal corresponds to one connected component. The time complexity is O(V+E) because we visit each vertex and edge exactly once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack/queue. This connects to the parent problem of graph connectivity analysis. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark vertices as visited when adding them to the stack/queue (leading to infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs properly, and incorrectly assuming the graph is connected. Real code pattern: Initialize visited array, iterate through vertices, for each unvisited vertex, perform DFS/BFS to mark all connected vertices as visited, increment component count.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: Vertex 0 unvisited → DFS(0): Mark 0 visited, explore neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited, explore neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited, explore neighbors: 1 visited. Return to main. components=1. Step 3: Vertex 1 visited, skip. Vertex 2 visited, skip. Vertex 3 unvisited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited, explore neighbors: 4 unvisited → DFS(4): Mark 4 visited, explore neighbors: 3 visited. Return to main. components=2. Step 4: Vertex 4 vis
... (simplified)

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: Vertex 0 unvisited → DFS(0): Mark 0 visited, neighbors: 1 unvisited → DFS(1): Mark 1 visited, neighbors: 0 visited, 2 unvisited → DFS(2): Mark 2 visited, neighbors: 1 visited, 3 unvisited → DFS(3): Mark 3 visited, neighbors: 2 visited. Return to main. components=1. Step 3: Vertices 1,2,3 all visited, skip. All vertices visited → return components=1.

testCases.input: n=100, edges=[[i,i+1] for i in range(99)]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal / Union Operation
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to visit all vertices in a connected component starting from a source vertex.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which naturally visits all vertices in a connected component when starting from any vertex within it. The algorithm uses recursion or a stack: mark the current vertex as visited, then recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. This ensures we traverse the entire component. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the component, but in practice bounded by the component size. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack. This feeds into the full problem by being called for each unvisited vertex to count components. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causing cycles), using incorrect adjacency list indexing, and stack overflow in deep graphs (use iterative DFS). Real code pattern: Define DFS function that takes vertex v, marks visited[v]=True, for each neighbor in adj[v], if not visited[neighbor], call DFS(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: DFS(0): Mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: [1] → 1 unvisited. Step 2: DFS(1): Mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: [0,2] → 0 visited, 2 unvisited. Step 3: DFS(2): Mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: [1] → 1 visited. Return recursively → visited=[True,True,True].
Step 1: DFS(3): Mark visited[3]=True. Neighbors: [4] → 4 unvisited. Step 2: DFS(4): Mark visited[4]=True. Neighbors: [3] → 3 visited. Return → visited=[False,False,False,True,True].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Counting / Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Loop through all vertices, initiating a new traversal for each unvisited vertex to count connected components.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that each connected component has at least one vertex, and by starting a traversal from each unvisited vertex, we ensure we discover all components exactly once. The core logic involves maintaining a visited array and a component counter. For each vertex from 0 to n-1, if it's not visited, we increment the counter and perform a DFS/BFS from that vertex to mark all vertices in its component as visited. This works because once a vertex is visited, all vertices in its component are visited, so we skip them in future iterations. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space is O(V). This connects to the full problem as the main driver routine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in vertex indexing, not resetting visited array, and incorrect component counting logic. Real code pattern: Initialize visited array of size n with False, components=0. For v in range(n): if not visited[v]: components+=1, DFS(v). Return components.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=[False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: v=0 not visited → components=1, DFS(0) marks visited=[True,True,True]. Step 3: v=1 visited, skip. v=2 visited, skip. Return components=1.

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False], components=0. Step 2: v=0 not visited → components=1, DFS(0) marks visited=[True,True,False,False]. Step 3: v=1 visited, skip. v=2 not visited → components=2, DFS(2) marks visited=[True,True,True,True]. Step 4: v=3 visited, skip. Return components=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list from number of vertices and edge list for an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an adjacency list efficiently represents a graph by storing, for each vertex, a list of its neighboring vertices. For an undirected graph, each edge (u,v) adds v to u's list and u to v's list. This representation allows O(degree(v)) access to neighbors, which is optimal for DFS/BFS. The algorithm initializes an empty list of lists of size n, then iterates through edges, appending v to adj[u] and u to adj[v]. Time complexity is O(E) to build, space is O(V+E). This connects to higher levels by providing the input structure for traversal. Common mistakes include not initializing adj correctly (e.g., using shallow copies), forgetting to add both directions for undirected graphs, and index errors. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]. For each edge [u,v] in edges: adj[u].append(v), adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Edge [0,1] → adj[0].append(1)=[1], adj[1].append(0)=[0]. Step 3: Edge [1,2] → adj[1].append(2)=[0,2], adj[2].append(1)=[1]. Final adj=[[1],[0,2],[1]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Visited Marking / Same Set Check
ladders.desc: Maintain and update a boolean array to track visited vertices during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to avoid reprocessing vertices and infinite loops in graph traversal, we need a mechanism to remember which vertices have been visited. A boolean array (or set) provides O(1) time to check and mark visited status. The operation involves initializing an array of size n with False, then setting visited[v]=True when vertex v is first encountered. This is fundamental because DFS/BFS relies on this to ensure each vertex is processed at most once. Time complexity for marking/checking is O(1), space is O(n). This connects to higher levels as the visited tracking system. Common mistakes include using wrong array size, not initializing properly, or modifying the array incorrectly. Real code pattern: visited = [False] * n. To mark: visited[v] = True. To check: if not visited[v]: ...

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False]. Step 2: Mark vertex 0 → visited[0]=True → [True,False,False]. Step 3: Mark vertex 2 → visited[2]=True → [True,False,True].

Step 1: visited=[False,False,False,False,False]. Step 2: Mark vertex 1 → visited[1]=True → [False,True,False,False,False]. Step 3: Mark vertex 3 → visited[3]=True → [False,True,False,True,False]. Step 4: Mark vertex 4 → visited[4]=True → [False,True,False,True,True].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Disjoint Sets
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, connectivity, and disjoint sets.
ladders.explanation: A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path. DSU groups vertices into disjoint sets efficiently for connectivity queries.

examples.output: Graph with 3 vertices and 2 edges forming a chain 0-1-2
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1) connects vertex 0 and vertex 1 bidirectionally. Step 3: Edge (1,2) connects vertex 1 and vertex 2 bidirectionally. Result: You can travel from 0 to 2 via 1.

examples.output: Graph with 3 vertices and 3 edges forming a triangle
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: A,B,C. Step 2: Edge (A,B) connects A and B. Step 3: Edge (B,C) connects B and C. Step 4: Edge (C,A) connects C and A. Result: Each vertex is connected to both others, forming a cycle.

testCases.expected: Graph with 2 vertices connected by 1 edge
Graph with 3 vertices, only 0 and 1 connected
Graph with 4 vertices forming two disconnected pairs
Graph with 3 vertices all connected in a triangle
Graph with 5 vertices in a line
Graph with 4 vertices, vertex 0 connected to all others
Graph with 5 vertices, edges: 0-4, 1-3, self-loop at 2
Graph with 6 vertices in a chain

approaches.name: Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
approaches.summary: Initialize each node as its own set, then union sets for each edge to merge connected components. The count is the number of distinct roots after processing all edges.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(α(n)) per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function

ladders.title: Full Problem — Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
ladders.desc: Given a graph with V vertices and E edges, count the number of connected components using Disjoint Set Union.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind using DSU for counting connected components is that initially each vertex is its own component. When we process edges, we union the sets containing the two vertices, effectively merging components. After processing all edges, the number of distinct roots equals the number of connected components. This works because DSU maintains equivalence classes where vertices in the same set are connected. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize DSU with V elements, each as its own set. 2. For each edge (u, v), call union(u, v). 3. Count distinct roots by iterating through all vertices and finding their roots. Time complexity is O(V + E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant). Space is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. This directly solves the problem by leveraging DSU's ability to merge sets efficiently. Common mistakes include not using path compression or union by rank, leading to worse time complexity, or incorrectly counting components by assuming each vertex's parent is the root (must call find). Real code pattern: class DSU with find and union methods, then iterate edges and count roots.

examples.trace: Initialize DSU with 5 sets: {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}, {4}. Process edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → sets become {0,1}, {2}, {3}, {4}. Process edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(0,2) → sets become {0,1,2}, {3}, {4}. Process edge [3,4]: union(3,4) → sets become {0,1,2}, {3,4}. Count roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=0, find(3)=3, find(4)=3 → distinct roots: 0 and 3 → count=2.

Initialize DSU with 4 sets: {0}, {1}, {2}, {3}. Process edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → sets become {0,1}, {2}, {3}. Process edge [2,3]: union(2,3) → sets become {0,1}, {2,3}. Process edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → find(1)=0, find(2)=2, union(0,2) → sets become {0,1,2,3}. Count roots: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=0, find(3)=0 → distinct roots: 0 → count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal / Union Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the union function in DSU that merges two sets efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for union is to merge two sets by attaching the root of one to the root of the other, using union by rank to keep the tree shallow. This ensures that find operations remain fast. Why it works: By always attaching the smaller tree under the larger tree (by rank), we minimize the height, which optimizes future find calls. Step-by-step: 1. Find roots of x and y using find (with path compression). 2. If roots are same, they're already in the same set. 3. Else, compare ranks: attach root with lower rank to root with higher rank; if ranks equal, attach one and increment its rank. Time per union is O(α(n)) due to find and constant-time attachment. Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the full problem by enabling efficient merging of components. Common mistakes: not using union by rank (leading to unbalanced trees), or not calling find to get roots before union. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rootX = find(x); rootY = find(y); if rootX != rootY: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY; else: parent[rootY] = rootX; if rank[rootX] == rank[rootY]: rank[rootX] += 1.

examples.input: Initialize DSU with 4 elements, parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. Union(0,1).
examples.trace: Find root of 0: 0. Find root of 1: 1. Roots differ. Compare rank[0]=0 and rank[1]=0: equal. Attach root of 1 to root of 0: parent[1]=0. Increment rank of 0: rank[0]=1. Result: parent=[0,0,2,3], rank=[1,0,0,0].

Find root of 2: 2. Find root of 3: 3. Roots differ. Compare rank[2]=0 and rank[3]=0: equal. Attach root of 3 to root of 2: parent[3]=2. Increment rank of 2: rank[2]=1. Result: parent=[0,0,2,2], rank=[1,0,1,0].

testCases.input: DSU with 3 elements, parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0], union(0,1)
DSU with 5 elements, parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0], union(0,4)
DSU with 2 elements, parent=[0,1], rank=[1,0], union(0,1)
DSU with 3 elements, parent=[0,0,2], rank=[1,0,0], union(0,2)
DSU with 4 elements, parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[2,0,1,0], union(1,3)
DSU with 1 element, parent=[0], rank=[0], union(0,0)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Counting / Find with Path Compression
ladders.desc: Implement the find function in DSU that returns the root of an element and compresses the path.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for find is to recursively traverse parent pointers until reaching the root, and then compress the path by making all visited nodes point directly to the root. This flattens the tree, making future finds nearly constant time. Why it works: Path compression ensures that after a find, all nodes on the path are directly connected to the root, reducing the height. Step-by-step: 1. If parent[x] != x, recursively set parent[x] = find(parent[x]). 2. Return parent[x]. Time is O(α(n)) amortized due to compression. Space is O(1) extra per call. This feeds into union and component counting by providing fast root lookups. Common mistakes: not using recursion or iterative method correctly, or forgetting to update parent pointers. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.trace: Start at 3: parent[3]=2 ≠ 3, so find(2). At 2: parent[2]=1 ≠ 2, so find(1). At 1: parent[1]=0 ≠ 1, so find(0). At 0: parent[0]=0, return 0. Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, return 0. Backtrack: set parent[2]=0, return 0. Backtrack: set parent[3]=0, return 0. Final root=0, parent=[0,0,0,0].
Start at 4: parent[4]=4, return 4. No compression needed. Root=4, parent=[0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation
ladders.desc: Initialize and maintain a parent array where each element points to its representative in a set.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to represent disjoint sets as trees, where each node has a parent pointer, and the root is the representative of the set. This allows efficient union and find operations. Why it works: By storing parent relationships, we can traverse up to find the root and merge trees by updating parent pointers. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an array parent of size V, where parent[i] = i (each element is its own root). 2. Optionally, maintain a rank array for union by rank. Time to initialize is O(V), space is O(V). This feeds into DSU operations by providing the data structure. Common mistakes: not initializing parent correctly, or using 0-based vs 1-based indexing inconsistently. Real code pattern: parent = [i for i in range(V)]; rank = [0] * V.

examples.trace: Create array of size 5. Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3, parent[4]=4. Create rank array all zeros. Result: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0].

examples.input: After some unions, parent=[0,0,2,2,4], rank=[1,0,1,0,0]. Update after union(0,2).
examples.trace: Find roots: find(0)=0, find(2)=2. Union: attach root 2 to root 0 since rank[0]=1 > rank[2]=1? Actually equal, so attach and increment. Set parent[2]=0. Since ranks equal, increment rank[0] to 2. Result: parent=[0,0,0,2,4], rank=[2,0,1,0,0].

testCases.input: V=5, initialize then union(2,3) then union(3,4)
V=3, initialize then union(0,1) then union(1,2)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Visited Marking / Same Set Check
ladders.desc: Given a DSU, determine if two elements belong to the same connected component by comparing their roots.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in DSU, elements are in the same set if they share the same root. This is a fundamental operation used in union to avoid cycles and in component counting. Why it works: The root represents the set identifier, so equality of roots implies connectivity. Step-by-step: 1. Find root of element x using find. 2. Find root of element y using find. 3. Return true if roots are equal, else false. Time is O(α(n)) per find, space O(1). This feeds into union and component analysis. Common mistakes: comparing parent[x] and parent[y] directly without find, which may not be roots. Real code pattern: def same_set(x, y): return find(x) == find(y).

examples.input: DSU with parent=[0,0,1,2]. Check if 0 and 3 are in same set.
examples.trace: Find root of 0: find(0)=0. Find root of 3: find(3) → parent[3]=2, find(2) → parent[2]=1, find(1) → parent[1]=0, return 0 with path compression. Roots: 0 and 0 → equal → true.

examples.input: DSU with parent=[0,1,2,3]. Check if 1 and 2 are in same set.
examples.trace: Find root of 1: find(1)=1. Find root of 2: find(2)=2. Roots: 1 and 2 → not equal → false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Disjoint Sets
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, connectivity, and disjoint sets.
ladders.explanation: A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path. DSU groups vertices into disjoint sets efficiently for connectivity queries.

examples.input: Elements: {A, B, C, D}. Initially, each in own set: {A}, {B}, {C}, {D}. Merge sets of A and B.
examples.trace: Start: sets are {A}, {B}, {C}, {D}. Merge set containing A and set containing B. Since they are disjoint, combine into one set: {A,B}. Result: {A,B}, {C}, {D}.

examples.input: From above, merge sets of B and C.
examples.trace: Current sets: {A,B}, {C}, {D}. B is in set {A,B}, C is in set {C}. Merge these two sets: combine all elements → {A,B,C}. Result: {A,B,C}, {D}.

testCases.input: Elements: {a,b,c,d}. Start sets: {a},{b},{c},{d}. Merge a and c.
From above, merge b and d.
From above, merge c and b.
Elements: {x,y}. Start sets: {x},{y}. Merge x and y.
Elements: {1,2,3,4}. Start sets: {1},{2},{3},{4}. Merge 1 and 2, then 3 and 4.
